title: Revisiting Deep Learning Models for Tabular Data (FT-Transformer)
authors: Yury Gorishniy, Ivan Rubachev, Valentin Khrulkov, Artem Babenko (Yandex; MIPT; HSE)
tokenization_method: Feature Tokenizer: each input feature (numerical or categorical) is mapped to its own d-dimensional embedding token Tj = bj + fj(xj). For a numerical feature, fj is element-wise multiplication of the scalar value by a learned vector W_j^(num) in R^d; for a categorical feature, fj is a one-hot lookup into a learned table W_j^(cat) in R^{S_j x d}. A learned per-feature bias bj is added in BOTH cases. The k feature tokens are stacked, a learnable [CLS] token is prepended, L pre-norm Transformer layers run over the sequence, and the final [CLS] representation feeds a linear head.
feature_types_handled: numerical and categorical (mixed). No native handling of missing values (assumes imputed input) and no temporal/time-varying support.
token_granularity: per-feature: one token per input feature (whole-feature, value-aware). All numerical and categorical features become exactly one token each; sequence length k = number of features (+1 for [CLS]).
numerical_strategy: raw-scalar-times-learned-vector: T_j = b_j + x_j * W_j^(num), i.e. a single linear (affine, via the bias) embedding of the standardized scalar. NOT piecewise-linear, NOT periodic/Fourier, NOT binned. (Those richer numerical embeddings are the follow-up paper 'On Embeddings for Numerical Features', Gorishniy et al. 2022.)
categorical_strategy: learned embedding lookup table per categorical feature (one-hot index into W_j^(cat) in R^{S_j x d}), plus a learned per-feature bias. Equivalent to classic entity embeddings but unified into the same token space and dimensionality d as numerical tokens.
high_cardinality_handling: none specific: each category gets its own row in the per-feature lookup table, so the embedding-table size grows linearly with cardinality S_j. No hashing, target encoding, or shared/compositional codes. High-cardinality fields inflate parameters and memory.
temporal_handling: none. Treats each row as an unordered bag of static features; no patching, recurrence, or time fusion.
scales_to_many_features: Limited and explicitly flagged by the authors. Sequence length equals feature count, and vanilla multi-head self-attention is O(k^2) in the number of features k, so the authors state the model 'may not be easily scaled to datasets when the number of features is too large' (hardware/time bound). They suggest efficient-attention approximations or distillation as mitigations but do not implement them. Benchmark feature counts are mostly small-to-moderate (8-136), with two high-dimensional outliers (Epsilon 2000 num. features, Yahoo 699) where the quadratic cost bites.
downstream_model: Transformer encoder (pre-norm vanilla Transformer layers, MHSA + FFN), ~930K params at default config; final [CLS] token to a linear classifier/regressor.
pretraining_objective: none; trained fully supervised end-to-end. No self-supervised or target-aware objective shapes the tokens.
key_results: Across 11 public datasets (objects 20K-1.2M; num. features 6-2000; cat. features 0-8) under one shared tuning protocol (Optuna): FT-Transformer ranks best on most tasks (mean rank ~1.8) and is the only DL model that no competitor consistently beats; ResNet baseline is the next strongest DL model (rank ~3.3). NODE is competitive on a few tasks but is ensemble-like and larger. FT-Transformer's gains over ResNet concentrate exactly on datasets where GBDT beats ResNet (CA, Adult, Covertype, Yahoo, Microsoft), i.e. it is a more 'universal' DL model. Synthetic interpolation between GBDT-friendly and DL-friendly targets confirms FT-Transformer stays competitive across the whole range while ResNet degrades on GBDT-friendly targets. Overall conclusion: no universally superior solution between best DL and GBDT.
baselines_compared: DL: MLP, ResNet (their tuned baseline), SNN, NODE, TabNet, GrowNet, DCN-V2, AutoInt; GBDT: XGBoost, CatBoost. Ablation against AutoInt and against FT-Transformer-without-feature-biases.
transferable_idea: The unified per-feature affine tokenizer Tj = bj + fj(xj): every heterogeneous feature (any numerical, any categorical) is projected into one shared d-dim token space with its own learned weight AND its own learned additive bias, then handled identically by a permutation-invariant Transformer. This is the canonical, minimal scaffold for tokenizing dozens-to-hundreds of mixed features; the per-feature bias is empirically necessary, and the numerical branch is the obvious slot to upgrade (PLR/periodic embeddings) for 70+-feature settings.
limitations: Authors-stated: more compute/time than ResNet; quadratic MHSA cost in feature count limits scaling to very wide tables; higher CO2. Implied: no missing-value handling, no temporal handling, no high-cardinality compression, supervised-only (no transfer/pretraining), embedding-table size linear in categorical cardinality.
pitfalls: 1) The much-cited 'FT-Transformer' is often conflated with the richer numerical embeddings (PLR/periodic) — but THIS paper's numerical embedding is just scalar*vector+bias; the powerful numerical embeddings are a separate 2022 follow-up. Do not assume strong numerical encoding from this paper alone. 2) The 'universality' claim rests on 11 fixed single-split datasets with one tuning budget; later benchmarks (e.g. Grinsztajn 2022) argue GBDT still beats tuned DL on many tabular tasks, so the 'no universally superior solution' framing should be read as best-case-for-DL, not DL-wins. 3) Scaling to 70+ (esp. high-cardinality) features hits the explicitly-acknowledged O(k^2) attention wall and linear embedding-table growth — the architecture as published is not the answer for very wide tables; you need efficient attention / feature selection. 4) Attention-map feature importances (sec 5.3) are presented as a cheap signal but the paper itself notes they are weaker/less reliable than Integrated Gradients.
relevance_to_topic: Foundational anchor for the topic: it defines the per-feature mixed-type token (the building block every later tabular-transformer tokenizer extends), but its naive scalar numerical embedding and O(k^2) attention are exactly the two things that must be replaced/augmented to reach the 70+-heterogeneous-feature, partly-temporal target setting.
code_available: yes — official PyTorch repo https://github.com/yandex-research/tabular-dl-revisiting-models and the packaged rtdl library (rtdl.FeatureTokenizer, rtdl.FTTransformer).
